g.title: Final Assembly of the LED Matrix Panel
g.desc: This diagram shows the completed assembly of the tombstone-shaped front panel. You can identify this panel by its large 8x8 grid of square cutouts and the word 'PIPER' engraved at the bottom. The panel is secured to a backing piece using three brass screws: one located above the grid on the upper right, and two located at the bottom corners flanking the brand name. When oriented correctly, the 'PIPER' text is at the bottom, and the sides of the panel taper inward toward the flat top edge.

g.title: Inventory List for the LED Light Show
g.desc: This inventory list includes the following components: one electronic LED matrix board featuring a grid of small bulbs; two strips of double-sided foam tape; a laser-cut wooden kickstand; two flat, rectangular wooden panels identified as 'Front' and 'Back'; a set of flexible jumper wires; four 5/8-inch long brass screws; and four small hexagonal brass nuts.

g.title: Applying foam tape to the LED matrix
g.desc: Locate the LED matrix, which is a flat, square circuit board. Identify the back of the board by feeling for several small, raised rectangular components and a plastic connector with a row of pins along one edge. Find two long, thin, squishy foam tape strips. Peel the adhesive backing from the strips and stick them onto the back of the board. Place them parallel to each other, one on each side of the central components, ensuring they are positioned away from the row of connector pins.

g.title: Positioning Supports Away From Central Chips
g.desc: This detail inset shows the correct placement of two large, dark-colored angled supports onto the square circuit board. The board has a row of five metal pins along its top edge and two small, raised rectangular chips in its center. Position one support on the left side and the other on the right side of the board. Ensure that neither support overlaps or covers the two small rectangular chips in the center.

g.title: Attaching the Kickstand with Screw and Nut
g.desc: This step involves a large wooden main plate with multiple rectangular cutouts and a smaller rectangular kickstand piece featuring T-shaped notches along one edge. Identify the kickstand by its notches and the main plate by its size. To assemble, position the kickstand perpendicularly against the back of the main plate, aligning one of its T-shaped notches with the small hole near the center of the plate. Slide a small hexagonal nut into the horizontal cross-slot of that T-notch. Insert the long brass screw through the hole from the front of the main plate and thread it into the nut to lock the pieces together.

g.title: Fastening Plywood with Bolt and Nut
g.desc: This cross-section diagram shows how to secure a piece of plywood using hardware. Identify the bolt by its long threaded shaft and flat head. The acorn nut is a small metal part with a six-sided hexagonal base and a smooth, rounded dome on top. The instructions show the bolt being pushed up through a hole in the 5mm plywood from the bottom. The acorn nut is then placed on the threaded end sticking out from the top and tightened.

g.title: Securing the LED Matrix between Panels
g.desc: This step involves sandwiching the electronic LED matrix between two wooden panels. The front panel is a plywood frame with a large rectangular cutout. The LED matrix is a square circuit board with a grid of small LED bumps on one side and metal pins on the top edge. The back panel matches the arched outer shape of the front panel but includes a vertical slot. Orient the matrix so the side with bumps faces through the front panel's window. Align the three small holes on the corners of the panels and the matrix board. From the front, slide three brass screws through these holes. Secure the assembly by twisting a small hexagonal brass nut onto the end of each screw on the back side.

g.title: Detail of Five-Pin Jumper Wire Connection
g.desc: A close-up view shows a black rectangular connector at the end of five jumper wires being attached to five metal pins. The connector is a small, solid block that you can feel with five small holes on its bottom edge. Locate the five thin metal pins on the electronic component, which are arranged in a horizontal row. Line up the holes on the connector with these pins and push it down firmly until it is secure. From left to right, the pins correspond to the functions GND, MOSI, CE, CLK, and VCC. Note that the colors of the five wires coming out of the top of the connector may vary from the image.

g.title: Final Assembly of the Piper Controller
g.desc: The drawing shows the completed device. It features a wooden front panel shaped like a rectangle with a rounded bottom and a tapered, house-like top with clipped corners. In the center is an 8-by-8 grid of square components that can be felt as a textured area. Below the grid, the word PIPER is engraved between two brass screws at the bottom corners. A third brass screw is located just above the top right corner of the grid. This front panel is securely mounted onto a thicker base.
